Party_Details.Name: Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd is a Singapore-incorporated company referred to as AT.
Prior to his death on 25 April 2008, Peter Fong was the majority shareholder and Chairman of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd.
Peter Fong was Chairman of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd until his death on 25 April 2008.
Linda Kao Chai-Chau was appointed managing director of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd in 1996 by Peter Fong.
Carolyn Fong is a shareholder of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd and held approximately 6.2% of its shares at the date of Peter Fong's death.
By 2006, approximately 51% of the shares in Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd had been transferred to the Fong Foundation charitable trust.

At the date of Peter Fong's death, he held approximately 3.6% of the shares in Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd, the Fong Foundation held about 51%, and the remaining shares were held by 11 other persons including family members.

Between around 2000 and the date of the writ in Suit 477 of 2012, business transactions involving Chinese chemical companies and Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd took place which are later described as business opportunities.
From around 2001, business transactions involving another Chinese company and Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd took place which are later described as business opportunities.
Around 2002 and 2004, further business-related acts involving Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd occurred which are later described as business opportunities.

While Peter Fong was alive and Chairman, various transactions involving Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd were carried out in which funds were received and disbursed by Linda Kao Chai-Chau in connection with business opportunities.

After the death of Peter Fong on 25 April 2008, further transactions and events involving Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd occurred in which business was conducted by or through Linda Kao Chai-Chau and other persons.
On 17 January 2012, Ernst & Young was appointed as Receivers and Managers of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd pursuant to an application by shareholder Carolyn Fong.

After their appointment on 17 January 2012, the Receivers and Managers of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd reviewed company affairs and identified transactions and diversions of business involving Linda Kao Chai-Chau and others which they considered detrimental to Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd.

On or before 2011, Carolyn Fong applied in Originating Summons No 505 of 2010 for leave under section 216A of the Companies Act to commence a derivative action on behalf of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd against Linda Kao Chai-Chau.

In 2012, Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd, acting through shareholder Carolyn Fong, commenced Suit No 477 of 2012 as a derivative action against Linda Kao Chai-Chau concerning transactions mostly occurring while Peter Fong was alive.

In 2012, the Receivers and Managers of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd commenced Suit No 1015 of 2012 against Linda Kao Chai-Chau and 15 other individuals and corporate entities concerning transactions and events mainly occurring after the death of Peter Fong.
In Suit No 1015 of 2012, the Receivers and Managers described a pattern in which business was diverted away from Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd by Linda Kao Chai-Chau and others.

In both Suit No 477 of 2012 and Suit No 1015 of 2012, Linda Kao Chai-Chau stated that the transactions in question were carried out in accordance with instructions or an understanding with Peter Fong.

In relation to certain transactions, Linda Kao Chai-Chau stated that she received and disbursed funds on behalf of Peter Fong's friends and associates as a favour to them, acting on Peter Fong's instructions.

In relation to other transactions where there were no specific instructions from Peter Fong, Linda Kao Chai-Chau stated that she acted pursuant to standing instructions or an understanding with Peter Fong.
In both suits, Linda Kao Chai-Chau stated that she had been authorised by Peter Fong to act as she did in relation to the impugned transactions.
In Suit No 1015 of 2012, the Receivers and Managers described a course of conduct in which Linda Kao Chai-Chau and 15 others diverted business away from Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd.

On 19 April 2013, in Suit No 1015 of 2012, Linda Kao Chai-Chau issued a third party notice against the Estate of Peter Fong seeking an indemnity in respect of any liability to Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd.
By Summons No 2990 of 2013 in Suit No 477 of 2012, Linda Kao Chai-Chau applied for leave to serve a third party notice against the Estate of Peter Fong seeking an indemnity.

By Summons No 2989 of 2013 in Suit No 1015 of 2012, the Receivers and Managers of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd applied on 12 June 2013 to set aside the third party proceedings commenced by Linda Kao Chai-Chau against the Estate of Peter Fong.

On 26 August 2013, an application was made to amend the third party notice in Suit No 477 of 2012 to add an alternative claim for contribution against the Estate of Peter Fong and to state that Peter Fong, as a director of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd, owed the same duties as Linda Kao Chai-Chau.

Summons No 4420 of 2013 sought to amend the third party notice in Suit No 1015 of 2012 to add a claim for contribution against the Estate of Peter Fong in addition to the existing claim for indemnity.
Summons No 4419 of 2013 sought to amend the third party notice in Suit No 477 of 2012 to add a claim for contribution against the Estate of Peter Fong in addition to the existing claim for indemnity.

Registrar's Appeal No 247 of 2013 was filed by Linda Kao Chai-Chau in Suit No 1015 of 2012 against the Assistant Registrar's decision setting aside the third party notice against the Estate of Peter Fong.

Registrar's Appeal No 246 of 2013 was filed by Linda Kao Chai-Chau in Suit No 477 of 2012 against the Assistant Registrar's decision refusing leave to commence third party proceedings against the Estate of Peter Fong.

The Estate of Peter Fong, represented by its own legal advisers, did not participate in Suit No 477 of 2012 or Suit No 1015 of 2012 and did not apply to set aside the third party notices or object to the commencement of third party proceedings.

After the appeals on the third party proceedings were heard, Carolyn Fong applied for leave to hand over conduct of the derivative action in Suit No 477 of 2012 to the Receivers and Managers of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd.
In the application to hand over conduct of the derivative action, the Receivers and Managers of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd took a neutral position.

At the hearing on 2 September 2013, the court requested further submissions on whether, as a matter of law, a director who follows instructions of a chairman or director regarded as the controlling mind of the company would have any liability if those instructions are followed blindly.
On 11 October 2013, counsel for the parties delivered further submissions on the legal question concerning the effect of following instructions from a controlling director.
In written submissions, Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd acting through Carolyn Fong accepted that Peter Fong had at least ostensible authority to give instructions on behalf of the company.

In written submissions, Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd acting through Carolyn Fong stated that Peter Fong did not know of certain transactions in question and or that those transactions were unauthorised.

In written submissions, the Receivers and Managers of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd denied that Peter Fong was the alter ego or controlling mind of the company in relation to the matters raised in Suit No 1015 of 2012.
In submissions, Linda Kao Chai-Chau described Peter Fong as the controlling mind and will of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd during the period when he was Chairman and majority shareholder.

Suit No 477 of 2012 is described as the Derivative Action and Suit No 1015 of 2012 is described as the RM Action; the plaintiffs in these suits are referred to as AT (Carolyn) and AT (RM) respectively.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, on the basis of pre-dispute conduct and corporate structure, Linda Kao Chai-Chau can seek indemnity or contribution from the Estate of Peter Fong in respect of the same financial losses and business diversions that are the subject of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd's claims in Suit No 477 of 2012 and Suit No 1015 of 2012.
Party_Details.Rule: Under section 15(1) of the Civil Law Act, any person who is in respect of damage suffered by another person may seek contribution from any other person who is also in respect of the same damage, whether the liability is joint or several and regardless of whether the basis of liability is tort, contract, of trust or otherwise. Under section 16(2) of the Civil Law Act, the court has discretion in contribution proceedings to determine the amount recoverable, including the possibility of a complete indemnity or an exemption from contribution. Procedural rules in Order 16 of the Rules of Court govern when and how a defendant may issue a third party notice to claim contribution or indemnity, and allow the court to terminate third party proceedings where appropriate.
Party_Details.Application: Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd, through shareholder Carolyn Fong and through its Receivers and Managers, commenced two related suits against managing director Linda Kao Chai-Chau concerning transactions and business diversions occurring both before and after the death of Chairman Peter Fong. Linda Kao Chai-Chau responded that she acted in accordance with Peter Fong's instructions or an understanding with him, and she initiated third party proceedings against the Estate of Peter Fong seeking indemnity and later contribution in respect of any liability for the same losses claimed by Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd. The company, acting through AT (Carolyn) and AT (RM), opposed the third party proceedings on the basis of redundancy, illegality, and procedural concerns, while the Estate of Peter Fong did not object. The factual matrix includes Peter Fong's role as Chairman and significant shareholder, the transfer of a majority shareholding to the Fong Foundation by 2006, the existence of multiple shareholders including family members, and the pattern of transactions and business opportunities involving Chinese counterparties over several years.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judgment analyses whether illegality bars a statutory contribution claim, interpreting sections 15, 16 and 19 of the Civil Law Act in light of English authorities such as K v P and local authority Nganthavee, and concludes that the ex turpi causa principle does not prevent a contribution claim under the statute. The court distinguishes between common law indemnity based on agency and statutory contribution, and reasons that the statutory wording is deliberately broad, covering liability for the same damage regardless of whether the underlying conduct is tortious, contractual, a breach of trust, or criminal. The judge considers the Assistant Registrar's view that Linda could not claim indemnity or contribution if she had the requisite knowledge for liability, and rejects the proposition that illegality automatically precludes a statutory contribution claim. The judgment then addresses whether the third party proceedings are redundant because they overlap with Linda's defence, examining Canadian cases Yemen and Westcoast, and the Singapore decision in Nganthavee, where third party proceedings were struck out as unnecessary when the third party's conduct would already be attributed to the plaintiff. The court also refers to Panweld, where a third party claim against another director was considered to have no merit because, if that director had agreed to the impugned payments, the defendant director would not be liable to the company in the first place. Applying these authorities, the judge reasons that in this case there is a realistic scenario in which Linda could be found liable to Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd for breach of director's and fiduciary duties even if she proves that she acted with Peter Fong's knowledge and consent, particularly if the trial court concludes that Peter Fong was not the alter ego or controlling mind of the company at the material times, or that his consent did not absolve her independent duties as managing director. The judgment explores the concept of the 'controlling mind' or 'alter ego' of a company, citing Lim Leong Huat and academic commentary, and notes that where a company sues its own directors for breach of duty, the directors' fraudulent state of mind is not imputed to the company. The court also discusses shareholder consent as a means of authorising directors' conduct, referencing Panweld, and finds that on the shareholding structure of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd, with the Fong Foundation holding 51%, Peter Fong holding 3.6%, Linda holding a smaller stake, and other family shareholders including Carolyn, it is not inevitable that all shareholders would have consented to the transactions in issue. The judge concludes that the third party notices are not redundant because there is a plausible outcome in which both Linda and Peter Fong (through his estate) could be liable in respect of the same damage, thereby justifying a contribution claim. The court acknowledges submissions by AT (Carolyn) and AT (RM) about potential delay, expense, and complexity arising from third party proceedings, but finds that the close connection between the main actions and the third party claims favours allowing the proceedings so that all issues can be resolved together. The judgment notes that under section 16(2) of the Civil Law Act, the trial court retains discretion at the end of the trial to adjust or even reduce to zero any contribution by the Estate of Peter Fong, for example to avoid unjust enrichment of Linda or to reflect considerations related to illegality. The judge decides that it is more appropriate for this discretionary assessment to be made after full evidence is heard rather than at the interlocutory stage. On this reasoning, the court allows Registrar's Appeals Nos 246 and 247 of 2013, sets aside the Assistant Registrar's orders that had prevented or terminated the third party proceedings, and grants Summonses Nos 4419 and 4420 of 2013 to amend the third party notices to include claims for contribution in addition to indemnity. The judgment reserves the question of costs to be determined later.

Party_Details.Name: Kao Chai-Chau Linda

Facts.Text: Kao Chai-Chau Linda is an individual who served as managing director of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd.
Linda Kao Chai-Chau was appointed managing director of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd in 1996 by Peter Fong, the then majority shareholder and Chairman.
From 1996 up to 2012, Linda Kao Chai-Chau acted as managing director of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd and was involved in managing its business and transactions.

During the period when Peter Fong was Chairman and majority shareholder, Linda Kao Chai-Chau carried out various transactions for Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd in which funds were received and disbursed.

In relation to certain transactions later questioned in Suit No 477 of 2012, Linda Kao Chai-Chau stated that she received and disbursed funds on behalf of Peter Fong's friends and associates as a favour to them, acting on Peter Fong's oral instructions.

For other transactions where there were no specific instructions from Peter Fong, Linda Kao Chai-Chau stated that she acted pursuant to standing instructions or an understanding with Peter Fong regarding how to handle such matters.

In both Suit No 477 of 2012 and Suit No 1015 of 2012, Linda Kao Chai-Chau stated that she had been authorised by Peter Fong to act as she did in relation to the impugned transactions and business opportunities.

In both suits, Linda Kao Chai-Chau stated that the business opportunities described as diverted were not business opportunities of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd but were handled in accordance with Peter Fong's directions.

In Suit No 1015 of 2012, Linda Kao Chai-Chau was named as a defendant together with 15 other individuals and corporate entities in relation to transactions and events mainly occurring after the death of Peter Fong.

In Suit No 477 of 2012, Linda Kao Chai-Chau was named as the defendant in a derivative action brought on behalf of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd concerning transactions mostly taking place while Peter Fong was alive.

In Suit No 477 of 2012, Linda Kao Chai-Chau filed a defence in which she relied on the position that the transactions complained of were carried out pursuant to Peter Fong's instructions or understanding.

In Suit No 1015 of 2012, Linda Kao Chai-Chau filed a defence in which she relied on the position that she had been authorised by Peter Fong to act as she did in relation to the transactions and diversions of business.

By Summons No 2990 of 2013 in Suit No 477 of 2012, Linda Kao Chai-Chau applied for leave to serve a third party notice against the Estate of Peter Fong seeking an indemnity in respect of any liability to Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd.

On 19 April 2013 in Suit No 1015 of 2012, Linda Kao Chai-Chau issued a third party notice against the Estate of Peter Fong seeking an indemnity in respect of any liability to Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd.

On 26 August 2013, Linda Kao Chai-Chau applied to amend the third party notice in Suit No 477 of 2012 to add an alternative claim for contribution against the Estate of Peter Fong and to state that Peter Fong, as a director of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd, owed the same duties as she did.

By Summons No 4420 of 2013, Linda Kao Chai-Chau applied to amend the third party notice in Suit No 1015 of 2012 to add a claim for contribution against the Estate of Peter Fong in addition to the existing claim for indemnity.

By Summons No 4419 of 2013, Linda Kao Chai-Chau applied to amend the third party notice in Suit No 477 of 2012 to add a claim for contribution against the Estate of Peter Fong in addition to the existing claim for indemnity.

Registrar's Appeal No 247 of 2013 was filed by Linda Kao Chai-Chau in Suit No 1015 of 2012 against the Assistant Registrar's decision setting aside the third party notice against the Estate of Peter Fong.

Registrar's Appeal No 246 of 2013 was filed by Linda Kao Chai-Chau in Suit No 477 of 2012 against the Assistant Registrar's decision refusing leave to commence third party proceedings against the Estate of Peter Fong.
In submissions, Linda Kao Chai-Chau described Peter Fong as the controlling mind and will of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd during the period when he was Chairman and majority shareholder.

In submissions, Linda Kao Chai-Chau acknowledged that the trial court might decide that her duties as managing director were owed to Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd notwithstanding Peter Fong's position as majority shareholder and Chairman.

In submissions, Linda Kao Chai-Chau acknowledged the possibility that the trial court might not regard Peter Fong as the alter ego or directing mind of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd for the purposes of the impugned transactions.

In submissions, Linda Kao Chai-Chau acknowledged the possibility that even if Peter Fong were regarded as the alter ego of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd, this might not be sufficient to absolve her from liability for her conduct as managing director.

In submissions, Linda Kao Chai-Chau stated that she sought third party proceedings against the Estate of Peter Fong so that, if she were found despite acting on his instructions, she could seek indemnity or contribution from his estate.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on her role as managing director and her stated reliance on Peter Fong's instructions or understanding, Linda Kao Chai-Chau can maintain third party claims for indemnity or contribution against the Estate of Peter Fong in respect of any liability she may incur to Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd for the same losses and business diversions.
Party_Details.Rule: Section 15(1) of the Civil Law Act provides that a person who is in respect of damage suffered by another may recover contribution from any other person who is also in respect of the same damage, whether the liability is joint or otherwise and regardless of whether the legal basis is tort, contract, of trust or otherwise. Section 16(2) of the Civil Law Act gives the court discretion in contribution proceedings to determine the amount recoverable, including the possibility of a complete indemnity or an exemption from contribution. Order 16 of the Rules of Court sets out the procedure for issuing third party notices by a defendant who seeks contribution or indemnity, and allows the court to terminate third party proceedings where they are unnecessary or would cause undue delay or prejudice.
Party_Details.Application: Linda Kao Chai-Chau, as managing director of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd, was involved in transactions and business opportunities that later became the subject of Suit No 477 of 2012 and Suit No 1015 of 2012. She has consistently stated that she acted in accordance with Peter Fong's oral or standing instructions or an understanding with him, and that the business opportunities in question were handled on that basis. Anticipating that the trial court might nonetheless hold her responsible for losses to Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd, she initiated third party proceedings against the Estate of Peter Fong in both suits, first seeking indemnity and later seeking contribution as well, in respect of any liability for the same damage. The statutory framework on contribution allows a person who is for damage to seek contribution from another person who is also for the same damage, and the procedural rules permit a defendant to bring in a third party for this purpose, subject to the court's control over redundancy, delay, and complexity.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court considers Linda Kao Chai-Chau's position that she acted on Peter Fong's instructions or understanding and that, if she is nonetheless held liable to Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd, she should be able to seek indemnity or contribution from his estate. The judgment examines whether public policy on illegality prevents such a claim, distinguishing between common law indemnity based on agency and statutory contribution under section 15 of the Civil Law Act. Relying on authorities such as K v P and Nganthavee, the court concludes that the ex turpi causa principle does not bar a statutory contribution claim, even where the underlying conduct may involve civil or criminal wrongdoing. The judge then addresses whether Linda's third party claims are redundant because they mirror her defence that she acted on Peter Fong's instructions. Drawing on Yemen, Westcoast, Nganthavee, and Panweld, the court notes that third party proceedings can be unnecessary where the third party's conduct is already fully reflected in the defence and where, if the defence succeeds, there is no remaining basis for contribution. However, the court identifies a realistic scenario in which Linda could fail in her defence yet still have a viable contribution claim: the trial court might find that Peter Fong was not the alter ego or controlling mind of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd at the material times, or that his consent did not relieve Linda of her independent duties as managing director, while also finding that Peter Fong, as Chairman and director, was himself liable in respect of the same damage. In that scenario, both Linda and the Estate of Peter Fong could be liable for the same losses, and section 15 of the Civil Law Act would permit Linda to seek contribution. The judgment also notes that the shareholding structure, including the Fong Foundation's 51% stake and the presence of multiple family shareholders, makes it uncertain whether full shareholder consent can be inferred for the transactions in question, distinguishing the case from Panweld where two shareholders could be treated as having given unanimous consent. The court recognises that the trial judge will later have discretion under section 16(2) of the Civil Law Act to adjust or even eliminate any contribution by the Estate of Peter Fong, for example to avoid unjust enrichment or to reflect considerations related to illegality, but decides that this discretionary assessment should be made after the full trial. On this basis, the court allows Linda's appeals against the Assistant Registrar's orders that had prevented or terminated the third party proceedings, and grants her applications to amend the third party notices to include claims for contribution as well as indemnity.

Party_Details.Name: Estate of Peter Fong

Facts.Text: The Estate of Peter Fong represents the legal interests of Peter Fong after his death on 25 April 2008.
Before his death, Peter Fong was the Chairman of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd and was previously its majority shareholder.
By 2006, Peter Fong had transferred the majority of his shares in Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd to the Fong Foundation charitable trust, which then held approximately 51% of the shares.
At the date of his death on 25 April 2008, Peter Fong held approximately 3.6% of the shares in Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd.

The Fong Foundation charitable trust held approximately 51% of the shares in Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd at the date of Peter Fong's death, with the remaining shares held by 11 other persons including family members.
While he was Chairman and majority shareholder, Peter Fong appointed Linda Kao Chai-Chau as managing director of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd in 1996.
During his tenure as Chairman, Peter Fong gave oral instructions to Linda Kao Chai-Chau regarding the handling of certain transactions and funds involving Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd.
In relation to some transactions, Peter Fong instructed Linda Kao Chai-Chau to receive and disburse funds on behalf of his friends and associates as a favour to them.
Peter Fong and Linda Kao Chai-Chau had an understanding or standing instructions concerning how certain categories of transactions and business opportunities should be handled.
Peter Fong was involved in decisions relating to business opportunities connected with Chinese chemical companies and other Chinese counterparties during the period before his death.
Peter Fong managed the affairs of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd in a manner that led directors and shareholders to acquiesce in his management while he was alive.

After his death on 25 April 2008, the Estate of Peter Fong did not participate in Suit No 477 of 2012 or Suit No 1015 of 2012, despite being named as a third party in third party notices issued by Linda Kao Chai-Chau.
The Estate of Peter Fong did not apply to set aside the third party notices or object to the commencement of third party proceedings in either Suit No 477 of 2012 or Suit No 1015 of 2012.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on Peter Fong's role as Chairman and his instructions and involvement in the transactions at issue, the Estate of Peter Fong can be joined as a third party from whom Linda Kao Chai-Chau may seek indemnity or contribution in respect of any liability to Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd for the same losses.
Party_Details.Rule: Section 15(1) of the Civil Law Act allows a person who is in respect of damage suffered by another to recover contribution from any other person who is also in respect of the same damage, regardless of whether the liability arises in tort, contract, of trust or otherwise. Section 19(2) of the Civil Law Act defines a person as in respect of damage if the person who suffered the damage is entitled to recover compensation from that person, whatever the legal basis. Order 16 of the Rules of Court permits a defendant to issue a third party notice against a non-party from whom contribution or indemnity is claimed, and gives the court power to terminate such proceedings if they are unnecessary or would cause undue delay or prejudice.
Party_Details.Application: Peter Fong, as Chairman and a significant shareholder of Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd, appointed Linda Kao Chai-Chau as managing director and gave her instructions and standing directions regarding the handling of certain transactions and business opportunities, including transactions involving Chinese counterparties and funds handled on behalf of his friends and associates. These transactions later formed part of the subject matter of Suit No 477 of 2012 and Suit No 1015 of 2012. Linda Kao Chai-Chau has sought to bring the Estate of Peter Fong into both suits as a third party from whom she may seek indemnity or contribution in respect of any liability for the same losses claimed by Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd. The Estate of Peter Fong, although represented, has not opposed being joined and has not applied to set aside the third party notices.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court evaluates whether the Estate of Peter Fong can properly be joined as a third party for the purposes of indemnity or contribution in light of Peter Fong's role and involvement in the impugned transactions. The judgment reasons that if, at trial, Peter Fong is found to have been involved in directing or authorising the transactions that caused loss to Airtrust (Singapore) Pte Ltd, he may be liable in respect of the same damage as Linda Kao Chai-Chau, thereby satisfying the statutory requirement under section 15(1) of the Civil Law Act for contribution. The court notes that the Estate of Peter Fong has not objected to the third party proceedings, and that the objections come instead from the plaintiffs in the main actions, who argue redundancy, illegality, and potential delay. The judge concludes that there is a sufficient factual and legal basis to allow the Estate of Peter Fong to remain as a third party so that, if the trial court finds that Peter Fong was liable in respect of the same damage, it can then consider any contribution or indemnity claim by Linda under the statutory framework. The judgment emphasises that the ultimate allocation of responsibility between Linda and the Estate of Peter Fong, including any adjustment or exemption under section 16(2) of the Civil Law Act, is a matter for the trial court after full evidence is heard.
